author: @janetizzy6741
text:
More notes on the home laboratory.
Measurement is one of the cornerstones of science and engineering.

General rules of thumb: 

In applied chemistry (and electronics) you want to work with three significant figures.
In analytical and research chemistry, five (or more) significant figures is the rule.

The "Jeweler's Balance" our channel host mentions is most often a three significant figure device. Say, 10 grams maximum in 0.1 gram increments.
The electronic kitchen scale will be either two or three significant figures. Read the label.
Check with your local police auction of impounded property, both of these devices are often used in the street drug trade.

The enclosed balance in the background that our channel hosts mentions is (at least) a five significant figure device.

A triple-beam balance is almost always a three significant figure device.

Avoid spring scales like the plague.

Glassware:

You need to watch your expenditures in this area, it is very possible to spend a lot of money on glassware.
So much so that you will not be the first to think that you should have taken up glass blowing rather than chemistry.

There is a very well established secondary market for chemistry glassware, both as physical stores and as Internet stores.
Large laboratories will on a regular basis turn over everything that isn't broke to a dealer and bulk order a new set of whatever they happen to use.
Laboratory glassware breaks, but (with rare exceptions) does not wear out.
So the key phrase here is: Shop and Save.

Volumetric flasks and beakers, if not labeled or labeled as "Approximate" are approximate.
Approximate being two significant figures (sometimes better).

Graduated cylinders are usually three significant figures.

Graduated pipettes are usually three (or more) significant figures.

The key here is to look for a: "at xx temperature" on the etched label.
Measurement of liquids to three significant figures almost always requires consideration of its temperature.

Everything you use does not have to be calibrated. This is another possible savings in expense.
General purpose glass containers as working containers are just fine, but you do need a way to make calibrated measurements, if only to put marks on that old wine bottle you are using.

For known liquids, instead of measuring its volume, you can always weigh it and convert to an equivalent volume.
This can be another possible savings in expense for large volumes.

Glassware comes in thin wall (1mm) and heavy wall (2mm) types.
Get the more expensive heavy wall type when you have a choice, you will be surprised at how easily the thin wall stuff breaks.

Glassware comes in heat resistant ("Pyrex") and non-heat resistant types.
You don't need the more expensive heat resistant type unless your heating or cooling its contents or the reaction generates heat.
But on the other hand, many people choose to purchase the more expensive, heat resistant type for its extra strength (I.E: more break resistant).
Note: "Home Canning Jars" are almost always of heat resistant glass.

A note on storage:

Plastic is often a better choice than glass.
Pay attention to what the chemical originally shipped in. Use similar material containers for storage.

"Glass" (other than very special types), has metal ions which can leach into your solution.

Storing a solution of Graphene Oxide is one of the special cases.
Metallic ions will make it clump.  Probably not what you want to happen.
Polystyrene, Polypropylene, Poly-anything is a better storage choice in this case.

author: @janetizzy6741
text:
You will want not just access to water, but a dedicated place to wash glassware and things.
Bargain hunters: 
Look for the old style, modified concrete, "laundry sinks" -
large, heavy, now considered ugly, (and hence cheap) but inert to nearly anything you can think of.

Chemical resistant work surface (on the cheap) -
Pool tables (a.k.a: billiard tables)
Large, very heavy, and when worn beyond use, expensive to recover.
For those reasons, you can often find one for a "You move it, you can have it" deal.
Remove remaining felt, the slate bed is very acid resistant.

Note: they can be disassembled into manageable pieces.
Organize a "Table Moving Party" among a number of your friends with promises of food and favorite drink - it will be worth the effort.

author: @stevesimpson5994
text:
If you're really tight on bench space, your might be able to hinge the bench-tops.  Bottom layer could be the bashing-bench, next layer could be the "flat" bench, top layer could be the glass bench..

Another one if you're absolutely desperate for space is making yourself rolling benches, ie. benches on quality castors.  You pack them in your 8ft sq. lab till "lab-day" and then roll out the ones you don't need on that day.  There's a lot of obvious problems with this approach, not the least of which is the weather..  But if you're story is starting with "I can't do X because" try of figure out ways of turning that into a "can" even if it's a "I couldn't do X, but I did do Y which helped a little"

I also like the idea of smoke alarms (someone made the comment below), but the false-positives might be a problem.
